data.title: Why An Investment Process Matters, Especially In Volatile Markets
data.description:
Markets have had a strong run year-to-date. But with those highs come some familiar concerns, such as valuations, volatility, and whether the rally can keep going. Benjamin Gossack, Managing Director, Co-Head of Global Equity Portfolio Management at TD Asset Management, discusses the benefits of having a consistent investment process, even if markets face uncertainty.

Transcript

Kim Parlee - Markets have had a strong run year to date, with the S&P 500 up about 16%. The NASDAQ up about 20%. But with those highs come some familiar concerns -- valuations, volatility, and whether the rally can keep going. My next guest says markets have already seen a big sell-off. And all the uncertainty out there highlights the importance of sticking to your investment process. Ben Gossack is with TD Asset Management, and he joins me now. It is lovely to have you here.

Benjamin Gossack - Yeah, it's a pleasure. Thank you for having me.

Kim Parlee - I feel like that -- what I just said, which came from the feedback you gave my producer, was directed to me directly because I know I get scared off. When you see markets so high, you just think it's too much. It's too much. So maybe just tell us a bit of what you're hearing about why people are feeling so uneasy about these market highs.

Benjamin Gossack - Yeah, it seems if we're at new highs, how could we go higher? We can only retrace what happened. And that seems to be what consistently is coming up in conversations as I go across Canada. But how are we supposed to get to higher highs once we're at these levels? So I think so much has happened this year. It's just been a barrage of news and various themes.

And I think people forget that we already had a 20% drawdown this year, and meanwhile everyone's expecting and waiting for the next shoe to fall. And we've cleared out a lot of concern in terms of policy back in spring.

Kim Parlee - Let's take a look at the chart. As always, thank you. You bring us lots of fascinating charts to look at. So this is a look at the S&P 500, the drawdown. And yeah, to your point, there has been a drawdown.

Benjamin Gossack - There was a major drawdown. So it took 33 days, so about a month, for the market to swiftly fall 21% and then about 56 days for us to recover that high. We did have a bit of a wobble within the past two weeks where we did fall about 5%, and there were so many different narratives as to what was driving that. And NVIDIA (NVDA) delivered record earnings, but then the market sold off. So was that the end of AI, and is it a bubble? And yet now we're probably 1% off those highs.

But again, if we did have a massive drawdown in a swift period because there was uncertainty about policy, and when markets got some clarity about what trade and how this administration was going to go about it, we did rally through that. But I think it's just that people have already forgotten that. And now we're waiting, "Well, if we're at highs, then...

Kim Parlee - It's going to happen again.

Benjamin Gossack - ...what's going to be the next pothole for the market?"

Kim Parlee - Let me ask you, you were saying that you believe that some of the pullbacks we've seen weren't driven by fundamentals, to your point, but you're saying these are our technical dynamics. Is this just the concerns about policy or other just uncertainty out there?

Benjamin Gossack - So I think markets are complicated because we have a whole bunch of people expressing their opinions, and then it all gets merged together, and we get a clearing price, and that's the price of a stock. And it changes every second throughout the day. We do spend a part of our process. We love fundamentals and secular trends and understanding human behaviors. But an equal part of that process is understanding patterns. And one pattern that we keep seeing, especially in this instance of the market, is we're seeing a double-top pattern.

So a stock or a sector had made a high. It had come off. It attempted to make another high and rolled over, or it broke out, and it failed to hold the breakout. And so people, when they see that pattern, they're like, "Oh, we're ready to go. Let's get all of our purchases going in." And then when that pattern doesn't hold, everyone's caught on the wrong side. And the outcome is a very vicious sell-off.

Kim Parlee - Can we bring up -- because I think, again, you talk about failed breakouts. We have a chart that shows that. So maybe just let's bring it up and talk a bit about what this is.

Benjamin Gossack - Right. So obviously, the key topic in the market is AI. And do we have a bubble? And is it NVIDIA's chip? Is it Google's chip? A whole bunch of stories. What people are looking at right here is the technology sector of the S&P 500. And I've taken that sector, and I've divided it by the market. All that people need to understand about this chart is if the chart is going up, it means that technology has relative strength to the market. And if it's going down, it means that technology has relative weakness to the market.

And so we have this red line. So that identifies the previous high, which happened in 2024. Tech did actually underperform for almost 10 months. Most people did not notice that. And then we actually rallied through and surpassed last year's relative high. But we failed. And so we had this vicious sell-off.

It does coincide conveniently with NVIDIA delivering earnings, but that wasn't good enough for the market. And that allowed a whole host of bearish opinions to sound really credible. And they could be credible. But it's the viciousness, I think, of a failed breakout that makes those bearish tones sound very credible. But yet it could have easily just been a technical sell-off, Kim.

Kim Parlee - It's interesting, too, because we talk about discipline investment process. And this isn't motherhood. This is about sometimes understanding your own behavior and how you get in your own way.

Benjamin Gossack - Yes, I do think prices drive sentiment. When I first started in this industry, I used to think there was a catalyst. You'd try to figure out what's driving the stock, and that would drive the behavior behind a stock movement. But really, I do think prices move. And then we as humans start to build the rationale for why it happened and how we feel about that.

And with social media and the speed at which I'm seeing the markets move faster and faster, one week, we're talking, it's got to be this, and the next week, we're flipping, and we're saying it's got to be this. And I think for the average person, that sounds very contradictory. It sounds confusing.

And this is why we really do preach having a process. How is it that you go about managing money? How do you think that you create value with your process? Because right now, we're just getting bombarded from all sides, and that process will at least -- it's sort of that compass where you're like, okay, I don't know where we are. We're in the middle of the ocean. But that compass helps guide you through, because no one is really trying to help you make money. They're going to try to confuse you as much as possible.

Kim Parlee - There is no shortage of AI information out there. I feel as though I'm almost drowning in it all the time to understand what's out there. What's your take on it from your perspective? What do you pay attention to?

Benjamin Gossack - So there's a lot of takes. There's a lot of people providing content. They think AI is going to do this or do that. I've tried to go more glass half full. Who would have imagined we'd be going into strangers' cars in 1990, 2000 as we go through the tech bubble?

So the iPhone doesn't come until 2007, and that wasn't discussed back then. So I think AI can do a lot of things. And I've just been taking it one day at a time in terms of how do I really think it's going to change the world, but it is having implications today in terms of stock prices and valuations.

Kim Parlee - Okay, so let's talk about that. There's been lots of different ways it does. But you've brought four charts of what's catching your attention. And one of these is you talk about how AI is reshaping the software world. How?

Benjamin Gossack - So everyone's focused on who has the best chip and who's got the best accelerator and who's got the best fiber optic cables. And what people may not be noticing is that software has been a casualty of this AI trend. For the longest time -- I used to cover technology starting in 2013. There was this moniker that software eats the world.

So we may have a breakthrough in hardware. So there was an iPhone. There's the desktop computer. But all the profits ended up with the software companies because they developed the interface that provided us value. Office suites provided productivity to the enterprise. And so we may not know exactly what AI does, but we do know it codes very well.

And so if it can code me tax accounting software, well, do I need to pay all those subscriptions to the major tax accounting software companies or legal software or customer relationship management? And so right now, the market doesn't know who the winner is and who's going to survive. And so they've decided to step away from the software sector.

Kim Parlee - And that's what we're seeing here in this chart; just this fall to the right is just to say, we're just not sure.

Benjamin Gossack - Right. So this chart is basically a tug of war between software and the S&P 500. And you see this rollover, which means it's -- imagine the S&P 500 just really pulling software to the other side and just saying, "We are not comfortable being in this space." And that, I think, is a big paradigm shift because it's been very easy from a technology investor perspective to just own software companies and avoid hardware.

Kim Parlee - Yeah, it's sobering. Okay, the other thing that's been happening, of course, is there's been so much emphasis on the Mag Seven and chips and NVIDIA, as you mentioned, and power, which, of course, is powering AI. And now there's the emergence of the anti-AI trade. So what is that?

Benjamin Gossack - So yes, I think there has been too much emphasis on the Mag Seven, and people have not counted other stocks that have outperformed. But as you said, yes, it's about power. It's about industrials. So my biggest challenge in terms of managing a US portfolio is I don't want to have a whole bunch of holdings, but they're all tied to one idea, which right now is AI.

And AI, like you said. In the past, if I wanted to get defensive, I'd go buy a utility. Well, right now, that utility is all about powering the data center. So the old paradigm is actually going to introduce more risk when I start reaching out for these sectors that would have helped me in the past.

What we are starting to see emerge right now is, yes, the market likes AI. It's exciting. But it also can introduce a lot of risk. So how do you properly add some defense to your portfolio? And we're starting to see emerge certain sectors, certain stocks where we can actually hedge that AI bet in that theme.

Kim Parlee - Let's bring up a few. The first one is health care.

Benjamin Gossack - Yeah, so health care has been a very challenging sector. So again, if we go back to our tug-of-war example, healthcare has lost the tug of war to the S&P 500. This is XLV. It's an ETF that represents the health care sector. So you have pharma, MedTech, drug distributors. They've just been losing. But we're starting to see that move because, well, there isn't a lot of AI right now in health care.

So we're seeing pharma companies move. We're seeing medical distribution companies. It's bringing back some life to this sector that most people had -- they had a lot of exposure to it, but because it's underperformed, they have really reduced their exposure. So there's a lot of opportunity here. And the one nice thing about health care -- it's not like -- I like banks. It doesn't matter really what bank I buy. They're all going to swim in the same direction. Every health care company is different.

So we're seeing a move. You still have to dig in and find the right companies, but it provides some diversity for the portfolio, which is very exciting right now.

Kim Parlee - Yeah, it is important and hard to find. We've only got about 30 seconds. So I want to make sure I get -- the next one is biotech. So I assume the same principle here.

Benjamin Gossack - Same principle, but even harder. So they're all moving. But you really have to know your biotech company. Because if you miss a phase trial, it can be disastrous.

Kim Parlee - Yeah. And then finally, utilities. But interestingly, these are not North American utilities. As you mentioned, these are EU utilities.

Benjamin Gossack - Yes, so North America is all about powering the data center, whether it's going to be nuclear or gas. In Europe, that's not even part of the discussion right now. So I'd say the European utilities are still acting like those traditional utilities that most investors would be familiar with when they're wanting to add some defense to their portfolio.

Original Post

data.title: $50 Oil Price Scenario: Enterprise Products Partners Better Positioned Than Energy Transfer
data.description:
ET and EPD stock: $50 oil price is a possible scenario

I last covered Energy Transfer LP (NYSE:ET) on Oct 28 with an article titled " Energy Transfer Q3 Preview: Graham P/E And Number Send Mixed Signals (Downgrade)”. The analysis served as an earning preview for the company’s 2025 Q3 earnings report (ER) and rated it as a hold. As for Enterprise Products Partners L.P. (EPD), my last analysis was published on Oct 17 under a title of " Energy Dividend Stocks: I Like Kinder Morgan, But Love Enterprise Products Partners”. That article presented a comparative analysis between EPD and KMI, and I rated EPD as a strong buy.

Since then, a few new catalysts have evolved around both stocks both in terms of macroeconomics and also company-specific updates. The top development in the former category is – obviously – the change in oil prices and more important the forward outlook. Oil prices have declined materially since my last writings and now hover around a 52-week low as you can see from the following chart. Looking ahead, a few headwinds could keep oil price pressured or cause it to go even lower. In particular, the latest announcement from OPEC+ (see this Seeking Alpha news for more details) suggest its production “will likely leave production levels unchanged” for 2027. In the meantime, China kept filling its strategic petroleum reserves. This serves as a strong supporting force for oil prices. It is so strong that softening in the country’s demand can pressure oil prices substantially. For example, a recent Wall Street Journal report projected that “If China really stops buying, the path toward low $50 would be very quick”. As detailed in an earlier article, I do see several factors that could China’s demands to weaken such as its struggling real estate sector.

In terms of company-specific updates, both EPD and ET have released their ER for FQ3 2025 since my last writings and also have made their latest dividend declarations. As to be detailed next, judging by these updates, I believe EPD is better positioned than ET should the scenario of lower oil prices materialize.

ET and EPD stock: latest dividend declarations reflect growth pressure

Given the central role of dividends for both stocks, let me start with the latest dividend information for both companies. As seen in the first chart below, EPD continues the partnership's commitment to consistent growth and regular payouts after the Q3 ER. The most recent payout, with an ex-dividend date of October 31, 2025, was set at $0.545 per unit, which represents a year-over-year increase of 3.8% when compared to the $0.525 distribution from the October 2024 period.

ET also declared its last quarterly distribution recently, with $0.3325 per common unit and an ex-dividend date of November 7, 2025. This amount reflects a year-over-year increase of 3.1% compared to the $0.3225 distribution paid around the same time in 2024. Such a low single-digit increase partially reflects the pressure from oil prices as mentioned in my view.

But EPD is better positioned than ET

To better contextualize the above figures, the next chart shows their dividend growth rates in the past 3 and 5 years. As seen, both company have been averaging about 4% of CAGR for their dividend growth in the past 5 years. ET’s 3-year growth rate is distorted by its substantial dividend cut amid the COVID pandemic. In contrast, during an economic crisis as severe as the pandemic, Enterprise not only didn’t cut its distribution but also kept growing the payout (for 27 consecutive years and counting).

Enterprise’s above resilience is well rooted in its business model and financial strength in my view. As such, I expect the stock to weather the aforementioned energy uncertainness better than ET. EPD is well diversified both geographically and operationally in the sector in my opinion. It is involved in almost every shale play and does business in every segment of the midstream industry. It derives a large proportion of its gross operating income from fixed, fee-based contracts, which ensures stable cash flow regardless of fluctuations in commodity prices. In contrast, while ET also boasts significant contract coverage, its business model incorporates a greater degree of commodity exposure through activities like inventory optimization and complex fractionation in my view. Due to such exposure, ET often see profit margins compress more sharply when crude and natural gas liquids (NGL) prices decline, with its dividend cut during the pandemic serving as the more recent demonstration. As another illustration of their different sensitivity to oil prices, the next chart shows their quarterly revenues’ correlation with Brent Crude Oil Spot Prices. As EPD’s correlation is noticeably lower than that of ET on average (0.448 vs. 0.590).

Furthermore, EPD’s financials are also far more conservative than ET. EPD maintains one of the strongest credit profiles in the entire energy midstream sector to my knowledge (recently confirmed at A- at Fitch). Such a strong rating allows it to access capital at lower costs than ET and other peers, and its debt burden is relatively low to start with. As you can see from the following chart, its current leverage ratio hovers around 3.62x in terms of Debt-to-EBITDA ratio, demonstrably more conservative than Energy Transfer’s ratio of 4.17x. When times get tough, the ability to manage debt cheaply and operate with a significantly lower leverage ratio is a crucial competitive advantage in my model. Finally, if you recall from an earlier chart, EPD's current dividend payout ratio of ~81% is also far more conservative than ET's ~105%.

Other risks and final thoughts

Of course, ET is a solid choice in itself. As shown in the correlation chart above, the company’s revenues show a relatively moderate correlation to oils prices compared to other sub-sectors (say integrated players or service players). The stock also provides a higher yield (7.92% on a FWD basis) than EPD (6.69%), offering a more attractive current income and also a thicker buffer to make up for its relative weaknesses mentioned earlier.

Finally, both ET and EPD are quite attractively priced under current conditions. Given the pronounced role of their dividends in their return profile, I believe the PEGY ratio (the price-to-growth-yield ratio) the most fitting metric following Peter Lynch’s insight detailed in our earlier articles. To wit, the table below displays my estimates for ET and EPD’s PEGY ratios. Their FY1 P/E were taken with the latest consensus estimates and unit prices as of this writing. The growth rates were taken as the dividend growth rate from their latest declaration as aforementioned. With these inputs, ET’s PEGY ratio worked out to 1.14x and EPD’s ratio is 1.17x, both quite close to the ideal threshold of 1x.

To conclude, this comparison of ET and EPD was promoted mainly by two new catalysts since my last writings: A) oil price change and future outlook, and B) their Q3 ERs and latest dividend declarations. These developments have led me to see $50 oil price as a likely scenario in the near future and thus motivated me to look for energy stocks that can better weather commodity price pressure. My verdict is that EPD is the more resilient candidate in this regard thanks to its superior insulation from commodity price risk, its conservative capital allocation strategy, and its industry-leading balance sheet strength. These considerations have led me to adjust my rating for EPD to buy and maintain my hold rating for ET.

data.title: Chipotle: Market Overreaction Creates A Rare Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Introduction

Last time I covered Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG), I highlighted how their strong brand, asset-light business model, and robust cash position support the company’s domestic and international expansion despite recent headwinds.

With an overall solid report despite weakness in the environment due to tariffs and low consumer spending seen in the entire industry, I believe the current selloff is not justified, while Chipotle still continues to generate solid numbers and even ramped up their investment plans while the market focuses on near-term headwinds.

Internal Developments

CMG fell following the Q3 report due to a combination of factors including tariffs resulting in weaker margins and profits, as well as very soft sales growth, with the management citing the “persistent macroeconomic pressures” as a key reason behind this underperformance, which I’ll address below since it’s something affecting the industry as a whole, not a company-specific issue.

CMG’s free cash flow reached $1.22 billion over the first 9 months of 2025, representing an increase compared to the $1.16 billion from a 9M’24 despite the higher CAPEX. Also, despite the weakness in consumer spending, Chipotle still highlighted their plans to accelerate their expansion, with 350 to 370 new restaurants in 2026 (compared to 315 to 345 in 2025), as the company is undergoing a significant international expansion.

Financially, based on CMG’s latest report, we see a good position, with the current assets above the current liabilities, a sizable amount of cash and equivalents on hand but also rising debt as the company is expanding - all while coming with a very tiny equity compared to their market cap.

However, keep in mind that this is a relatively common thing for a company in this industry, as they invest billions into their brands which are not going to be reflected on the balance sheet, similar to McDonald’s (MCD) or even players from other industries like Nike (NKE), PepsiCo (PEP), etc., for which the brand plays a massive role but can’t be properly reflected on the balance sheet, which I believe is a limitation of current accounting standards that’s also visible especially for digital businesses like Alphabet (GOOG).

Ultimately, the brand’s value can be reflected on the balance sheet if it’s acquired (explained by goodwill/intangibles to some degree, but not perfectly), and it’s realistically impossible to properly calculate compared to real estate, for example.

Macro Developments

As mentioned, rate cuts will be a very important catalyst for Chipotle, especially as the company is going through an accelerated expansion stage. Any future reduction in the cost of debt plays a key role here, while these cuts can also improve their customers’ discretionary budgets, which is ultimately a main aspect of this tool, with hikes being used to slow down the economy to fight inflation and cuts doing the opposite.

Also important is the fact that Powell’s term will expire in May 2026, allowing Trump to nominate a more obedient Fed Chair this time, which can potentially come with some key implications for the general market. Although it’s true that the Fed Chair represents 1 vote out of 12 in the Federal Open Market Committee, this person still comes with huge influence on the market, being a very important spokesperson/representative of the US economy as a whole.

As we’ve seen especially in recent years with Powell, their views and opinions even outside of Fed meetings carry a ton of influence for the general market, so if Trump manages to name someone more obedient this time, there is potential to see a lot more confidence in the Fed’s next moves.

However, this also comes with risk that the market will definitely try to price in, as it can also mean that this person will be “forced” to listen to Trump rather than doing what the economy needs at the time, even though technically and legally the Chair isn’t a subordinate of the US President, which is a dangerous thing to play with. For example, listening to Trump’s calls for more aggressive rate cuts - like the equivalent of 10 cuts he asked for in mid-2025 - can rapidly deteriorate the US economy and the US Dollar.

And, keep in mind that these rate-generated improvements or a potential recovery in consumer/business spending won’t come overnight. Even for CMG, It can take several years to see these lower rates reflected in the company’s financials (as they’ll have to refinance the fixed debt) or until their customers benefit from them, as rate cuts by themselves aren’t the sole factor affecting treasuries/SOFR/etc.

The US Government recently going through its longest shutdown in history also amplified this fear and led to even more uncertainty, although we’ve recently seen some Fed officials signaling that they see the possibility of a December cut - which we’ll know for sure soon, as the Fed’s next meeting is scheduled for December 9 and 10.

Still, even if the environment was optimal for Chipotle and their clients, we’re still talking about a highly-competitive market. Sure, their specific Mexican food niche is less saturated compared to burgers or fried chicken (especially internationally), but these companies ultimately fight for the same clients and the same discretionary budgets. Simply put, if someone buys McDonald’s for lunch, they likely won’t buy Chipotle as well for the same lunch.

Especially since they are a relatively small player, they will have to be very careful in how they navigate this expansion and balance it with keeping/improving their solid image and quality of service, as there is definitely risk of seeing them focus too much on expanding and neglecting their quality/image, risking getting into a similar position to Wendy’s (WEN), for example.

Tariffs are also a very important factor especially given Chipotle’s niche, as tariffs especially on Mexico can hurt the avocado/tomato/beef/pepper/etc. market significantly. It’s true that Chipotle has a diversified supply chain and about half of their avocado came from Peru, Colombia, and the Dominican Republic, but these tariffs are ultimately going to have an impact on all of these commodities - even if they’re produced domestically.

Tariffs are even more important for Chipotle as they can affect not only the US, but also the other countries where CMG is trying to expand, so this is definitely a big aspect for the market that will likely continue to bring volatility. Also, continued political pressure and the anti-immigration situation in the US can just as well damage the industry as a whole, which is a risk to keep in mind that will likely be reflected in the data soon, as it can result in even worse rising labor costs and lower demand.

What these tariffs ultimately do is hurt the already-hurt lower-income consumer, with about 40% of CMG’s sales coming from low- to middle-income households (earning below $100,000 a year), which dropped sharply in Q3. This also reinforces the point about the company’s dependency on the economy, potentially benefitting from tariff resolutions and improvements in the economy.

Still, Chipotle made the decision to absorb the impact of tariffs during 2025, instead of letting the customers pay it as usual, while they still estimate to be 20% to 30% below competitors like Sweetgreen or Cava in terms of prices and maintaining solid margins, as the CEO also pointed out during the Q3 Earnings Report:

As a reminder, our value proposition includes food made fresh with the highest quality ingredients, prepared using classic culinary techniques, served in generous portions with reliable accuracy and fast, friendly service. Currently, all of this is delivered at a price point that is 20% to 30% below our peers. This gap has widened over the last few years as our pricing has consistently trailed the broader restaurant industry. In fact, our pricing has tracked more closely with food at home and food away from home. Bottom line, our value proposition has never been stronger.

So, even though CMG has significant potential especially internationally, there are plenty of risk factors that will likely continue to make the stock volatile, as the management will have to navigate a pretty uncertain environment with high debt costs, while also keeping a close eye on their quality of service, locations, products, and more.

Updated Valuation

For Chipotle’s valuation, I’ll go with a conservative starting FCF of $1.5 billion in 2025 for an extra margin of safety, going for a CAGR of 18% over the first 5 years followed by a 12.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years (which is below the expected EPS CAGR of ~18.5% during the entire decade, for example) since the company still has massive potential to expand both domestically and especially internationally, with plenty of high-growth markets to address, with a terminal growth rate of 4.5%. I’ll go for a WACC of 11% (well above my estimated 8.87% that assumes a ~9.3% cost of equity and ~5% cost of debt) to take into account the risk-reward CMG comes with and a great margin of safety, $3.56 billion in net debt, as well as 1.34 billion shares outstanding as of the Q3 report.

This way, we get to an enterprise value of $54.66 billion, an equity value of $51.10 billion and an intrinsic value of $38.13 per share, and keep in mind that we’re starting with a relatively weak year in terms of FCF given their potential and using lower expectations than the market, with a high WACC on top. Still getting above the current stock price even with these assumptions is quite clearly indicating that the current level is way too low, in my opinion.

Conclusions

Overall, CMG remains a Buy thanks to their significant potential to grow combined with an attractive valuation despite the near-term headwinds. They will ultimately depend a lot on the economy, so it’s possible to see more weakness and volatility because of it, but the long-term remains very solid thanks to a potential recovery and expansion.

data.title: DTCR: A 'Picks And Shovels' Play For The Digital Economy
data.description:
Global data center revenues are expected to grow dramatically over the next few years, from roughly $420 billion to over $600 billion in 2029, to support the growth of generative AI, mobile connectivity, smart grids, and other tech-based infrastructure.

The Global X Data Center & Digital Infrastructure ETF (DTCR) investors a targeted, globally focused way to participate in this trend. DTCR aims to capitalize on this secular growth by providing exposure to the essential "picks and shovels" needed to develop the underlying technologies.

Here are some of the positive aspects of investing in the data center sector:

  • Massive secular demand: There is currently explosive demand for data processing and storage driven by generative AI adoption, cloud service expansion, 5G rollouts, and the Internet of Things or IoT. Global data center revenues should see large growth to support this.

  • High Barriers to Entry: It is not easy to build new large-scale data centers. It is capital intensive and time consuming because of regulations, permitting, and construction costs. It is also heavily reliant on access to large supplies of reliable power. Existing operators have a big advantage over newcomers.

  • Stable revenue streams: Many data center operators and digital infrastructure companies rely on long term leasing contracts with AI "hyper-scalers" like Amazon AWS, Google Cloud, Microsoft Azure, and other major cloud providers using a pay-as-you-go recurring payment model. Often the payments are inflation-linked.

DTCR's Unconstrained Approach to Digital Infrastructure

DTCR's investment strategy is designed to capture the full breadth of the digital infrastructure buildout. The fund tracks the Solactive Data Center REITs & Digital Infrastructure Index, which provides a key differentiator: an unconstrained approach to the digital infrastructure value chain.

Unlike some peers that may focus solely on Data Center REITs, DTCR expands its scope to include:

  • Data Centers (REITs and operators).

  • Cellular Towers and Fiber Networks (the physical conduits for 5G and connectivity).

  • Digital Infrastructure Hardware and equipment companies that supply the critical systems (cooling, power, and high-density servers) within the centers.

This approach offers broader exposure, allowing the ETF to invest in essential companies globally without having to monitor for strict traditional sector or geographic boundaries. This allows them to capture growth where the digital infrastructure boom is most intense.

Morningstar Rating Overview

As of the latest data, the DTCR ETF has an overall 4-star Morningstar Rating based on performance. But its Morningstar Medalist is Negative because of weaknesses in its Process and People rating. Morningstar notes that DTCR does maintain a cost advantage over competitors and is priced within the least expensive fee quintile of its peers.

DTCR Sector Allocation Breakdown

Information Technology

51.83%

Real Estate

45.20%

Materials

2.97%

As a sector-based ETF, DTCR invests most of its assets in only two sectors- Information Technology and Real Estate.

DTCR Recent Performance

The DTCR ETF has had excellent performance since inception. Its peer group category on Morningstar is US Fund Real Estate.

Recent Distribution History

DTCR is more of a total return play than an income generator. It pays two distributions a year, and its 30-day SEC yield is 1.37%. Here are the distributions for 2024 and 2025.

ETF Valuation Metrics

I looked at the ETF valuation metrics on the Seeking Alpha Quant model. The overall Quant Rating is a BUY at 3.73. Here are the ETF grades for the five subcategories:

Note that the momentum rating is quite high compared to just a few months ago. Because of this, it might pay to leg into DTCR gradually and wait for a minor pullback before adding more exposure. The expenses rating is only C+ because DTCR's fee of 50 basis points is a bit higher than some simpler index ETFs. I am surprised that the model assigns a D+ Risk rating, but that is likely because DTCR is more of a sector-based ETF, so there is some concentration risk.

DTCR has the typical characteristics of a high-growth ETF:

  • P/E Ratio: Most of the top holdings are in the 25 to 30 range. This indicates that growth expectations are built into the price, which is typical for technology-focused investments.
  • Dividend Yield: Around 1.4%. This is modest but does provide some income to add to the expected capital growth.

Here is a brief assessment of the top five holdings as of early December. I've included the Seeking Alpha Quant model ratings.

1) 10.48% Equinix Inc. (EQIX) Quant Rating= 2.91

A global leader in co-location and interconnection data centers.

2) 9.97% Digital Realty Trust (DLR) Quant Rating= 3.27

One of the largest data center REITs.

3) 8.75% American Tower Corp. (AMT) Quant Rating= 2.45

Develops multi-tenant communications real estate, including cell towers and fiber.

4) 8.60% Applied Digital Corp. (APLD) Quant Rating= 4.96

Provides high performance computing for AI, machine learning and blockchain.

5) 7.09% Crown Castle Inc. (CCI) Quant Rating= 2.61

Major communications infrastructure REIT focused on cell towers and fiber option networks.

Some summary statistics for DTCR:

Factsheet

  • Total assets: 622.9 Million

  • Inception date: October 27,2020

  • Last Semi-Annual Distribution = $0.096707

  • ETF Expense Ratio: 0.50%

  • Beta versus S&P 500= 1.23

  • Beta versus Nasdaq-100= 0.86

  • Portfolio Weighted Average Market Cap= $182.1 million

  • Average Daily Volume (shares) = 476,776

  • Average Dollar Volume = $10 Million

Summary

DTCR uses the classic "picks and shovels" investment strategy, which may offer an advantage when investing in transformative technological revolutions like AI, cloud computing, and 5G, where things can change very rapidly.

It is often more reliable to invest in the companies that supply the essential tools—the shovels, wagons, and clothing—rather than trying to guess which gold miner will strike it rich.

In the context of the digital economy:

  • AI and Cloud Computing: Instead of trying to pick the single winning AI software platform or cloud application (the "gold miners"), DTCR invests in the Data Centers and Hardware Suppliers (the "picks and shovels") that all of the cloud and AI applications use.

  • 5G Connectivity: Similarly, the rollout of 5G and future mobile networks depends on the expansion of Cell Towers and Fiber Optic Networks. DTCR captures this fundamental layer of connectivity, which is critical to realizing the speed and low latency required for autonomous driving and advanced IoT applications.

This strategy offers some diversification and "defense" as it benefits from the aggregate capital expenditure and infrastructure demand from all of the major tech companies.

Some Potential Risks and Pitfalls

  • Electric Power Concerns: Data centers are significant consumers of electricity and water, leading to increasing regulatory scrutiny. There may also be potential public opposition complaining about the affordability of power. These factors can significantly delay or block new projects.

  • Interest Rate Sensitivity: Many companies in the sector are REITs, which often rely on debt financing for expansion. Their valuations and operating costs are sensitive to changes in interest rates.

  • Technology and Competition Risk: While barriers to entry are high, the technological landscape often changes rapidly. Operators must continuously invest in cutting-edge cooling and power solutions to stay competitive. They also face potential future competition from some of their own largest clients (hyper-scalers) who may build some of their own facilities.

  • High Concentration Risk: The top five holdings are almost 50% of the portfolio. If there were regulatory changes affecting any of these companies, there could be a significant impact on the ETF.

Conclusions

DTCR provides a timely and specialized vehicle for investors who want to own the essential infrastructure of a more digital future. While sector-specific risks, particularly concerning power consumption and interest rates, need to be monitored, the fund’s exposure positions it to potentially benefit from some of the most powerful long-term secular trends in the global economy.

DTCR is quite liquid. The bid-asked spread is usually only a penny. Limit orders should be used for larger orders, but smaller market orders should be fine especially when you get price improvement.

DTCR provides a reasonable way for less active investors to gain access to the data center and digital infrastructure sectors. Because the distributions are fairly low, it can be a good holding in a retirement account or a taxable account.

data.title: Microsoft: Probabilities Are Shifting Toward Danger
data.description:
We all know that the Mag 7 companies are currently driving the stock market higher, and Microsoft Corporation (MSFT)(MSFT:CA) being the 4th biggest company in the world – according to market capitalization – certainly has an impact on that overall performance. And Microsoft by itself is making up slightly above 6% of the S&P 500's entire market capitalization.

In March 2025, I wrote my last article about Microsoft, and I was once again not very optimistic about the stock, and in the conclusion of my article, I summed up my reasons to be not so optimistic at this point – from a micro (looking at Microsoft) and macro perspective (looking at the economy):

Over the long run, Microsoft is a good investment, and we are dealing with a great business. But considering the point at which we are in the business cycle and long-term debt cycle, we should be rather cautious. The high valuation multiples Microsoft is trading for are not only reason to think twice about an investment but are also a sign for most companies being at the end of the business cycle with sentiment being very bullish, which is leading to high valuation multiples. In my opinion, Microsoft remains a “Hold” at best.

Now we must admit that Microsoft performed very well since my last article was published, and the stock increased about 25% in value and also made new all-time highs. Of course, we can point out that Microsoft was the worst-performing stock among the Magnificent 7 in the last three years since the AI-rally started with the release of ChatGPT 3.5 in November 2022. However, Microsoft still increased 90% in value, which is a great performance, and my rather cautious attitude might have been a mistake.

With these statistics in mind, we look at Microsoft once again and check whether the reasons in my last article to be cautious are still up to date or if I must revise my position. At this point we can already spoil that I will remain cautious about Microsoft and don’t see many reasons to be bullish about Microsoft as the macroeconomic risks prevail and the stock is trading for even higher valuation multiples.

Quarterly Results

Despite remaining cautious, we can’t ignore that Microsoft continues to report great results. On October 29, 2025, the company reported first quarter results for fiscal 2026, and revenue increased from $65,585 million in the same quarter last year to $77,673 million in this quarter – resulting in an increase of 18.4% year-over-year. Operating income increased even 24.3% year-over-year from $30,552 million in Q1/25 to $37,961 million in Q1/26. And finally, diluted earnings per share increased from $3.30 in the same quarter last year to $3.72 in this quarter – resulting in 12.7% year-over-year growth.

Finally, free cash flow also increased from $19,257 million in Q1/25 to $25,663 million in Q1/26 – resulting in 33.3% year-over-year growth. And when looking at the bigger picture, we see that Microsoft has been growing at a high pace for many years now (with a short exception around 2023).

Looking at the different segments Microsoft is reporting in, all three contribute to growth, with More Personal Computing growing “only” 4.4% year-over-year to $13,756 million in revenue while operating income increased 17.8% year-over-year to $4,163 million, also resulting in an improved margin, which increased 340 bps year-over-year.

However, the biggest two drivers of growth were Productivity and Business Processes and Intelligent Cloud. The first segment of the two increased revenue 16.6% year-over-year to $33,020 million, and operating income increased 23.6% year-over-year to $20,407 million.

While Productivity and Business Processes is the most important segment as it is contributing the biggest part of revenue and operating income, Intelligent Cloud is reporting the highest growth rates, and revenue for the segment increased from $24,092 million in the same quarter last year to $30,097 million this quarter – resulting in 24.9% year-over-year growth. Operating income also increased 27.5% year-over-year to $13,391 million.

And finally, analysts also remain optimistic for the years to come and expect earnings per share to grow with a CAGR of 13% in the next ten years (between fiscal 2025 and fiscal 2035).

Risk: Economy Stumbling

Without much doubt, Microsoft is a great business and growing with a high pace at this point. When looking at the numbers, there is not much indicating that Microsoft is not in a great state. However, in my last article I mentioned risks I see for the economy. We can start by looking at the yield curve, which I did not mentioned in any article for quite some time now. Nevertheless, the yield curve is still indicating a potential recession as the two major metrics – the 10-year minus 3-month and 10-year minus 2-year treasury yield – fell below the recession threshold (turning into negative territory) and above it again. Or to put it differently: The yield curve inverted and re-inverted again. And after these two steps happen, a recession usually follows within the next few months.

We can also look at the labor market. But the labor market is still in very good shape. The unemployment rate ticked higher and is now at 4.4%, which is still a rather low unemployment rate. Initial claims were 215,000 in the last four weeks on average. This is an extremely low number and indicating a strong labor market and economy.

Usually, I would also pay attention to the housing permits. But this number has not been updated since August 2025, and therefore we are rather flying blind here and don’t really know whether the situation improved again or got worse. But a further declining number of housing permits would be another warning sign, as the housing market and construction is one of the first sectors to be affected in an economy that is slowing down.

Aside from the yield curve, the consumer sentiment (measured by the University of Michigan) is another important indicator and is sending strong warning signals. With a reading of 53.6, it is very close to the lowest it has ever been. We can make the argument that it has been low for quite some time, but I don’t know whether this is a counterargument or actually a harbinger of terrible news.

In my previous article about Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), I already mentioned that the economy in the United States is currently driven by the rich and I wrote:

This is especially true as spending in the United States is driven entirely by the rich. According to an analysis by Moody Analytics, spendings growth in the United States stemmed almost entirely by the rich since the pandemic in 2020 with the bottom 80% merely keeping up with inflation. This is going hand-in-hand with the wealthiest 1% increasing their share of overall wealth: In 1990, it was 22.8%, now it is 31.0% with the bottom 50% only possessing 2.5%.

Similar to the rich keeping up the economy and being responsible for a huge part of spendings, we see almost the same concentration among businesses (a few major companies spending heavily) and the stock market (a few stocks driving performance).

In the past, we often looked at the top 5 companies in the S&P 500, but in the last few years, it was especially the Magnificent 7 that got the most attention and were driving the performance of the S&P 500. When excluding the Mag7 stocks from the S&P 500, the index returned about 50% over the last 5 years, and while this is still a solid performance, the Mag 7 stocks increased 170% in the same timeframe.

And such an outperformance usually leads to an extreme index concentration. When looking at the top 10 companies (and stocks), they are responsible for almost one-third of the earnings of more than 500 stocks. Additionally, these ten companies make up 41.6% of the total market cap of the S&P 500. This is clearly exceeding the Dotcom bubble peak and is another warning sign for a potential bubble in the stock market.

All in all, I would be very cautious about the stock market and the economy at this point. And Microsoft might be part of a potential AI bubble we are in.

Risk: AI Bubble

In the last few months, the reports about AI potentially being a bubble increased. Of course, there are also people arguing we are not in a bubble. Especially worrisome is the question of how real the high demand for NVIDIA’s chips actually is or if AI is rather a self-inflating bubble and if a few major companies (including OpenAI and Microsoft) are just circulating money.

Several deals indicate that NVIDIA is creating its own demand, and OpenAI is also at the center of the web. Especially a deal between Nvidia and OpenAI in late September fueled the bubble speculation. Reuters was writing in an article:

The deal gives chipmaker Nvidia a financial stake in the world's most prominent AI company, which is already an important customer. At the same time, the investment gives OpenAI the cash and access it needs to buy advanced chips that are key to maintaining its dominance in an increasingly competitive landscape. Rivals of both companies may be concerned the partnership will undermine competition.

In my opinion, OpenAI is facing high risks if the construct should somehow collapse, and Microsoft, with its close ties to OpenAI, is also facing major risks. Of course, Microsoft is a huge company with a very healthy balance sheet and cash reserves. Hence, I think the company will be able to absorb shocks—even if OpenAI should run into serious trouble. Nevertheless, we should not ignore the risks.

Financial Engineering

Another problem we are seeing right now is the rise of financial engineering. Similar to the Great Financial Crisis, companies are trying to get risks of their own books and keep them away from the balance sheet. And while this might be good news for the major technology companies, it creates a huge risk for the overall economy, as credit risk, and the risk of default are suddenly widely spread across the economy.

One risk many market participants still underestimate is how quickly these risks can ripple through the economy. If some companies fail, some banks might run into trouble. If some banks are in trouble, depositors might get worried and start pulling funds, which can create liquidity problems for several banks. And if financial institutions have problems, they certainly won’t lend money to anyone, which makes it difficult to get loans, making it more difficult to invest. And when consumers as well as businesses are spending less, this leads to lower revenue – because the spending of businesses and people is the revenue and income of other businesses.

Especially the technology companies and those investing heavily in AI might be affected heavily. Recently, it was reported that Microsoft was cutting its software sales quotas tied to AI as several salespeople were not able to meet the sales targets. Microsoft is denying this, but if true, it might be another hint for a potential AI bubble bursting and the economy running into troubles.

Valuation Multiples Not Justified

A final problem are the high valuation multiples. Don’t get me wrong, Microsoft is growing at a high pace and certainly does deserve higher valuation multiples. However, at the time of writing, Microsoft is trading for 34 times earnings as well as 46 times free cash flow. And while the P/E ratio could be justified by looking at the growth rate in the teens, it is much more difficult to justify the P/FCF ratio.

Of course, when talking about the P/FCF ratio, we must mention that free cash flow is rather stagnant due to the high capital expenditures. This is a pattern we are seeing for all the major technology companies right now, which are spending heavily on cloud and AI infrastructure, which leads to extremely high capital expenditures.

Nevertheless, trading close to 50 times free cash flow seems very expensive for Microsoft at that point and this also becomes obvious when using a discount cash flow calculation to determine an intrinsic value for the stock. As always, we calculate with a discount rate of 10% and the last reported number of diluted shares outstanding (which were 7,466 million in Q1/26). As basis for our calculation, we can take the free cash flow of the last four quarters, which was $78,020 million. In order to be fairly valued right now, Microsoft must grow its free cash flow 15% annually followed by 6% growth till perpetuity.

In my opinion, 15% growth for the next ten years seems rather optimistic and is not a scenario I would like to bet on for Microsoft at this point. Margins are very stretched and we are close to the Dotcom bubble highs and Microsoft is among the top 20 companies in the S&P 500 with the highest margins. And without margins expanding, I don’t think Microsoft will be able to report such high-growth rates. In the last ten years, Microsoft was able to grow its bottom line with a growth rate above 20%, but this was mostly due to margin improvements.

I would argue that we should be rather cautious at this point and not calculate with a higher growth rate than 12% (and this is a very optimistic growth rate). Calculating with these assumptions, we get an intrinsic value of $399.24 for the stock. And if OpenAI should actually run into troubles and the economy will enter a recession, growth rates in the double digits seem rather unlikely in the next few years

Conclusion

I remain very cautious about Microsoft at this point and still see “Hold” as the best rating we can give at this point. And of course, it is possible that Microsoft will continue to rise, the major technology companies will continue spending heavily and AI adoption will increase. But as we always should think in probabilities, we must consider the risk of declining stock prices and the bubble popping and right now the risks outweigh the opportunities and upside potential.

data.title: U.K.'s Rising Debt Costs And Fiscal Uncertainty
data.description:
By Krzysztof Kamiński

The British economy is facing serious fiscal challenges that are increasing tensions in the bond market and may affect the pound’s exchange rate. Despite the government’s declarations about maintaining budget balance, experts warn that the tax increases announced by Chancellor Rachel Reeves are too dispersed to meaningfully strengthen public finances and ease pressure on debt markets.

A prospect of 6% yields

David Zahn of Franklin Templeton forecasts that the yield on 30-year UK government bonds could reach 6%, highlighting the government’s difficulties in financing rising expenditures.

The current spread between UK and German bonds of the same maturity stands at 176 basis points and 78 basis points relative to France, placing the UK among the most expensive developed countries in which to borrow.

Zahn, who sold all his UK bonds back in March, believes that only a further rise in debt-servicing costs will force the government into genuine fiscal adjustment. As he puts it, “Eventually yields will be so high that the government won’t be able to sweep the problem under the carpet any longer.”

Questionable revenue and political risk

Although Reeves has pledged to increase the “fiscal buffer” and avoided the most controversial tax hikes (such as taxes on pension withdrawals or capital gains), rating agencies and think tanks doubt the long-term stability of the plan.

November also saw the second-worst outflow from UK equity funds in history; investors withdrew £3 billion, reflecting market tensions ahead of the budget announcement. Only after the budget was published did the outflows stop, suggesting investors feared radical changes that ultimately were not introduced.

GBP: Short-term relief, long-term risks

The pound has recently appreciated against a weakening dollar, and the bond market calmed after the budget release. However, by the end of the European session, GBP/USD met resistance at the 200-period SMA, which also aligns with the 50% Fibonacci retracement of the decline seen between September and November this year. Structural issues within the UK economy - high debt levels, low growth, and weak investment sentiment - remain unresolved.

Meanwhile, investors continue to avoid UK funds - only one month out of the last 55 saw a net inflow of capital. The government is attempting to stimulate the market through measures such as tax incentives for new listed companies and changes to the ISA system to encourage equity investment.

Strong commodities and decorrelation in the energy market

Against the backdrop of fiscal problems, the commodities sector remains the one bright spot in the UK market - copper and gold prices are hitting records, supporting mining companies’ valuations. The FTSE 100 owes much of its strength to the rising weight of mining stocks in the index.

The energy sector (FTSE 350 Energy) has also seen gains recently despite falling oil prices, likely driven by high dividends and share buyback programmes. However, investors should be aware that valuations may not hold if the geopolitical situation improves - for instance, if peace is achieved in Ukraine. On Friday, the index recorded losses of more than 1.76%.

The UK is at a turning point. A relatively strong currency and buoyant commodities sector contrast sharply with deep concerns over the stability of public finances.

The prospect of rising bond yields and tax uncertainty may, in the coming months, once again increase the government’s financing costs and weaken investor confidence in the pound and UK assets.

Original Post

data.title: U.S. IPO Weekly Recap: December Kicks Off With 3 Notable Launches In A Week With 7 SPACs
data.description:
The US IPO market entered the final stretch of the year with a few small listings, a wave of SPACs, and three notable deal launches on the calendar for the week ahead (CDNL, LMRI, WLTH). AI developer Anthropic, last valued at $183 billion, sent shockwaves when news broke that it hired a law firm to help prep for a possible IPO late next year.

Three micro-cap IPO pricings

Dental services provider Park Dental Partners (PARK) priced its IPO at the midpoint to raise $20 million at an $87 million market cap. The diversified dental services network partners with dentists and others to provide oral surgery, periodontics, pediatric dentistry, prosthodontics, and other services in the Midwest. Park Dental Partners finished the week up 2%.

Singapore-based flooring materials distributor SMJ International Holdings (SMJF) priced its US IPO at $4.00 to raise $10 million at a $110 million market cap. The company sells and distributes carpet tiles, broadloom carpets, vinyl tiles, and other flooring projects in Singapore and 20 other countries, including Malaysia, Indonesia, and the Philippines. SMJ ended the week up 10%.

Israeli hydrogel implant developer Regentis Biomaterials (RGNT) also listed in the US this past week, raising $10 million at a $47 million market cap, as well as ginger-based soft drink maker Reed’s (OTCQX:REED), which completed its NYSE American uplisting, raising $10 million at a $46 million market cap; these valuations were too small to be included in our 2025 IPO stats, and are therefore excluded in the table below.

Seven new SPACs raise a combined $1.5 billion

New America Acquisition I (NWAXU) raised $300 million to target US tech, healthcare, and logistics companies; the SPAC is led by Kevin McGurn (T-Mobile, Vevo) and features advisory board members Donald Trump Jr. and Eric Trump. SilverBox Capital’s SilverBox V (SBXE.U) raised $240 million. Bitcoin Infrastructure Acquisition (BIXIU) raised $200 million, targeting digital assets. Maritime-focused General Purpose Acquisition (GPACU) raised $200 million. Activate Energy Acquisition (AEAQU) raised $200 million to target oil and gas. Safeguard Acquisition (SAC.U) raised $200 million to target aerospace and security. Leapfrog Acquisition (LFACU) raised $125 million to target energy and infrastructure outside of the US.

9 IPOs During the Week of December 1st, 2025

Blank check company targeting the digital asset industry.

Eight SPACs submitted initial filings. Avi Katz’s GigCapital9 (GIXXU) filed to raise $220 million. Art Technology Acquisition filed to raise $220 million, targeting tech and art. Hennessy Capital Investment VIII (HCICU) filed to raise $175 million, targeting industrials. Muzero Acquisition (MUZRU.RC) filed to raise $175 million, targeting technology-enabled businesses. Blue Water Acquisition IV filed to raise $125 million to target healthcare and tech. United Acquisition I (UACU) filed to raise $100 million, led by Globis Capital Advisors’ Paul Packer. Mammon Omicron Acquisition (OMICU.RC) filed to raise $60 million, led by Chinese executives. Also formed by Chinese executives, APEX Tech Acquisition (TRADU) filed to raise $60 million to target US companies.

10 Filings During the Week of December 1st, 2025

Blank check company led by semiconductor veteran Avi Katz.

Week Ahead

The 2025 IPO window is quickly narrowing, with just two weeks left in the year's typical IPO calendar. Three sizable offerings are scheduled for the week ahead, featuring fintech, healthcare, and infrastructure. With markets recovering from a recent sell-off in growth stocks, we expect several more launches from the pipeline to tee up listings for mid-December, including a potential mega IPO from medical supplies giant Medline (MDLN).

VC-backed robo-advisor Wealthfront (WLTH), raising $450 million at a $2.4 billion market cap, is likely next week's best-known deal. The automated investing platform serves more than 1.3 million clients with over $88 billion in platform assets, offering both investment advisory and cash management. First launched in 2011, its platform assets skyrocketed over the past three years as higher interest rates drove demand for cash management. The company highlights its Millennial and Gen Z customer base, asset retention rate, and profitability (39% operating margin). However, growth has decelerated recently, while cash spreads could be pressured by rate cuts. Cornerstones BlackRock and Wellington plan to take down one-third of the deal.

Backed by PE firm Welsh Carson, Lumexa Imaging (LMRI) is raising $463 million at a $1.8 billion market cap. Formerly known as US Radiology Specialists, the company operates 184 outpatient diagnostic imaging centers in the US, primarily in Texas and the Southeast, which provide MRIs and CT scans as well as mammograms, ultrasounds, and X-rays. The company has expanded significantly through acquisitions as well as opening de novo centers.

Cardinal Infrastructure (CDNL) aims to raise $242 million at a $769 million market cap. The largely executive-owned company provides wet utility installations (water, sewer, and stormwater systems) for buildings, along with related services. While it has significant exposure to the housing market in North Carolina (particularly Raleigh), that market has served it well so far, boasting strong growth and leading margins.

Several lock-ups are expiring in the week ahead, including four from companies that raised at least $50 million.

IPO Market Snapshot

The Renaissance IPO Indices are market cap-weighted baskets of newly public companies. As of 12/4/2025, the Renaissance IPO Index was up 7.7% year-to-date, while the S&P 500 was up 18.0%. Renaissance Capital’s IPO ETF (IPO) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Kenvue (KVUE) and Reddit (RDDT). The Renaissance International IPO Index was up 36.9% year-to-date, while the ACWX was up 30.6%. Renaissance Capital’s International IPO ETF (IPOS) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Galderma and Horizon Robotics.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 12/06/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: Markets Cool In November: Real Estate Takes The Lead
data.description:
November 2025 saw modest movements across most asset classes, with U.S. real estate staging a notable comeback, gaining +2.37% after October’s decline. Despite the monthly gain, U.S. real estate remains up 5.75% for the year.

Most asset classes posted small positive returns for the month. Bonds gained +0.60%, pushing their year-to-date return to 7.43%. Commodities added +0.26%, bringing their 2025 gain to 6.89%.

Managed Futures continued its recovery with a +0.24% increase, marking its fourth consecutive positive month, though still down -1.19% for the year. World stocks and U.S. stocks showed similar modest gains of 0.21% and 0.20%, respectively, with year-to-date returns of 29.28% and 17.62%.

Hedge funds experienced a slight decline of -0.10%, their first negative month since March, yet maintained a solid 7.70% return for 2025. Cash continued its reliable performance with a +0.32% gain, bringing its year-to-date return to 3.90%.

As the final month of 2025 closes, world stocks maintain their substantial lead in year-to-date performance, while most asset classes remain comfortably positive for the year.

Past performance is not indicative of future results.

Past performance is not indicative of future results.

Sources: Managed Futures = SocGen CTA Index

Cash - US T-Bill 13-week coupon equivalent annual rate/12, with YTD the sum of each month’s value

Bonds - Vanguard Total Bond Market ETF (BND)

Hedge funds - IQ Hedge Multi-Strategy Tracker ETF (QAI)

Commodities - iShares S&P GSCI Commodity-Indexed Trust ETF (GSG)

Real Estate - iShares U.S. Real Estate ETF (IYR)

World stocks - iShares MSCI ACWI ex-U.S. ETF (ACWX)

US stocks - SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY)

All ETF performance data from YCharts

data.title: MBB: Take Advantage Of This Infrequent Opportunity
data.description:
Thesis

Mortgages are not usually exciting. Agency mortgages even less. But once every decade, when rates are set to move lower aggressively, the space can become interesting. One of the largest ETFs in the MBS space is the iShares MBS ETF (MBB), which sports a $39 billion AUM (assets under management).

In today's article we are going to go through MBB's structure and composition and outline why we believe the fund to be a robust investment for 2026.

Fund composition - Agency MBS only

Let us start with the fund objective as present on its website:

The iShares MBS ETF seeks to track the investment results of an index composed of investment-grade mortgage-backed pass-through securities issued and/or guaranteed by U.S. government agencies.

The respective index is the Bloomberg US MBS Index, thus making MBB a passive ETF. The current fund composition is as follows:

  • Federal National Mortgage Association: 44.6%
  • Federal Home Loan Mortgage Corporation: 29%
  • Government National Mortgage Association: 24%
  • Uniform MBS: 1.2%
  • Cash: residual

Since all the holdings are Agency MBS bonds, they hold the same rating as U.S. treasuries and do not have credit risk. Other analytics are as follows:

  • WAC: 3.5%
  • Duration: 5.4 years
  • OAS: 33 bps
  • Convexity: -0.5

There are over 11,000 holdings in the ETF, and the fund comes with a 30-day SEC yield of 4.15%. As we are writing this article, 5-year treasuries are trading at 3.7%; thus, the fund provides for a spread over treasuries given the MBS product.

Mortgages are boring

Because they are essentially treasuries plus a spread, mortgages with an intermediate duration are fairly boring:

  • 2018: 0.8%
  • 2019: 6.2%
  • 2020: 4.0%
  • 2021: -1.4%
  • 2022: -11.7%
  • 2023: 5.0%
  • 2024: 1.3%
  • 2025: 7.9%

If we look at the historic returns for MBB, we get a sense of the 'excitement' around mortgages. A staggering +0.8% in 2018 will not provide for a good party conversation. Nor will the 'astounding' +1.3% return in 2024. Intermediate MBS bonds do not tend to do much unless one comes from a high rates environment. Like today's macro picture. 'Boring' also equates to a very low volatility, with the annualized volatility as per the Seeking Alpha 'Risk' tab at only 5.2%.

Macro picture - new Fed Chair might cut aggressively

In the United States, the Federal Reserve is the central bank, and it is mandated to implement monetary policy without interference from the executive branch (i.e., the President). The general idea is for the Fed to implement a monetary policy that brings long term stability rather than short term political objectives.

However, the President is the one who nominates the Fed Chair every four years, which is then confirmed by the Senate. Jerome Powell's term is set to expire in May 2026, opening the door for the President to nominate a new Chair. As per the latest political observations, Kevin Hassett, the chairman of the National Economic Council and long-time Trump adviser, is set to be announced as the new Fed Chair.

The market's view is that Kevin Hassett might embark on an aggressive Fed Funds cutting exercise in order to achieve political goals:

Dec 3 (Reuters) - Bond investors have expressed concerns to the U.S. Treasury that Kevin Hassett could aggressively cut interest rates to align with President Donald Trump's preferences, the Financial Times reported on Wednesday, citing several people familiar with the conversations.

While the Fed controls the front end of the yield curve and the intermediate portion to a certain extent, the bond market controls the long end. As we saw above, MBB's duration sits at 5.4 years, thus falling in the intermediate bucket, and is thus very much correlated with what the Fed does. If we look historically, we can see a very high correlation between Fed Funds and 5-year rates:

The correlation is very high during times when rates move lower or higher. 5-year rates tend to 'anticipate' to a certain extent Fed Funds, and front-run them. Please note how 5-year rates started moving up during 2022 before the Fed changed the monetary stance. Similarly, in 2025, 5-year rates anticipated Fed cuts. Once Fed Funds stabilize, there is a bit of a term premium (i.e., spread) between Fed Funds and 5-year rates.

What is the expected return for 2026?

We have shown you above the close correlation between Fed Funds and 5-year rates. The ultimate net result for 2026 will be an equation to be solved by the next Fed chair. However, MBB is correctly placed to take advantage of a Chair that is very aggressive. Let us see a couple of cases.

Base Case: cutting Fed Funds to 2.5%

If the Fed ends up cutting Fed Funds to 2.5%, and assuming a term premium of 50 bps, we get 5-year rates down to 3%. This means a move from roughly 3.7% to 3%. Given the ETF's duration, this translates into a capital gain of roughly 3.8%, which, when added on top of the yield, can provide for a total return in excess of 7.5% in the next 12 months.

Investors can permutate around with the neutral Fed Funds rate and the term premium, but for a fund like MBB, a total return in excess of 7% is not something we see very often. In fact, in the past decade we have only seen it once, and that is this year (2025 is on track to achieve a total return in excess of 7%).

The policymakers have been fairly transparent about wanting lower rates in order to tackle a soaring deficit and high costs of the U.S. debt, and while they do not own the long end of the curve, they can certainly lower the front end. MBB is an ideal 'boring' instrument to take advantage of that dynamic, and the fund is set to be able to clock another year of +7% in total returns.

Conclusion

MBB is a passive ETF that holds Agency MBS with an intermediate duration. The name has a low volatility profile and does not run any credit risk. The main returns driver here is represented by rates. MBB does best when the Fed cuts aggressively, and the potential nomination of Hassett might achieve just that. Given its 5.4-year duration and 4.15% SEC yield. We are penciling in a total return in excess of 7% for 2026 in our base case.
